This is Feynman’s Thinking Habit. It Made Him a Genius
Most people think intelligence is something you’re born with.
They believe that the smartest people simply see the world differently because they have bigger brains, better memories, or natural talent.
I believed that too.
Then I learned about Richard Feynman.
Feynman wasn’t just one of the greatest physicists of the twentieth century. He was also famous for solving problems in ways that looked almost effortless. People admired his curiosity, creativity, and ability to explain difficult ideas in simple language.
What surprised me wasn’t his IQ.
It was his habit.
A habit that almost anyone can learn.
Years ago, a student asked Feynman how he became so good at understanding complicated subjects. His answer wasn’t about reading more books or memorizing more facts. Instead, he talked about thinking.
He believed that knowing the name of something isn’t the same as understanding it.
That’s a mistake many of us make every day.
We read an article, watch a video, or listen to a podcast and feel smarter because the information sounds familiar. But familiarity is not understanding.
Real understanding begins when you can explain an idea without hiding behind technical words.
Feynman constantly tested himself with one simple question:
“Can I explain this so clearly that someone with no background could understand it?”
If the answer was no, he knew he hadn’t learned it yet.
That sounds simple.
It isn’t.
Try explaining how the internet works without using technical terms.
Or describe inflation without mentioning economics.
Or explain artificial intelligence to a twelve-year-old.
Suddenly, the gaps in your knowledge become obvious.
That’s exactly what Feynman wanted.
Instead of avoiding those gaps, he looked for them.
Because every confusing sentence pointed to something he still needed to learn.
I started using this habit while learning new skills online.
At first, I thought I understood topics like investing, writing, and productivity.
Then I tried explaining them in plain English.
Within minutes, I realized I had memorized ideas instead of understanding them.
That changed how I learned forever.
Now, whenever I finish reading a book or watching a lesson, I close everything and explain the main idea in my own words.
No notes.
No jargon.
Just simple language.
If I struggle, I know exactly what to study next.
This works far beyond science.
A teacher can use it to prepare better lessons.
A business owner can explain products more clearly.
A student can remember concepts longer.
A writer can create articles that readers actually enjoy.
Even everyday conversations become easier because you’re speaking from understanding instead of repeating information.
I’ve also noticed another benefit.
People trust simple explanations.
Many beginners think sounding intelligent means using complicated words.
The opposite is often true.
The people who truly understand a subject usually explain it with calm, clear language.
Think about your favorite teacher.
They probably didn’t impress you with difficult vocabulary.
They made difficult things feel easy.
That’s rare.
And valuable.
In countries like Pakistan, many students prepare for exams by memorizing textbooks because that’s how the education system often rewards them. In many Western countries, students are encouraged to discuss ideas more openly, but memorization still happens there too. No matter where you live, the challenge is the same: remembering information isn’t the same as understanding it.
This is why Feynman’s habit still matters today.
We consume more information than any generation before us.
Videos.
Podcasts.
Newsletters.
Books.
Social media.
The problem isn’t a lack of knowledge.
It’s the illusion that collecting information automatically makes us wiser.
It doesn’t.
Understanding only happens when we slow down, ask questions, and explain ideas in our own words.
So the next time you learn something new, don’t ask yourself whether you’ve finished the chapter.
Ask yourself a different question.
“Could I teach this to someone else using only simple words?”
If you can, you’ve probably learned it.
If you can’t, you’ve just discovered your next lesson.
That small habit turned Richard Feynman into one of history’s greatest thinkers.
It probably won’t make you a Nobel Prize winner.
But it can make you a better learner, a better communicator, and someone who keeps improving long after everyone else stops asking questions.
Sometimes, genius isn’t about knowing more.
It’s about understanding better.
